coredata.dc:title: Ion-imprinted CDs-Pc nanohybrid sensor for ratiometric fluorescence and electrochemical detection of Pd(II)
coredata.dc:creator: Arslan, Taner
Güney, Sevgi
Güney, Orhan
coredata.dc:description: We report here for the first time a novel dual-fluorescence emission and electrometric sensor based on palladium ion-imprinted organosiloxane nanoparticles (Pd-imp@CDs-Pc) grafted with both carbon dots (CDs) and phthalocyanine (Pc) dye for the determination of palladium ion, Pd(II) as a target. Nanoparticles showed two emission peaks at the double excitation wavelengths of 367 and 620 nm. These peaks were assigned to emissions induced by CDs (456 nm) and Pc (698 nm). The fluorescence of Pc embedded in nanoparticles is slightly quenched, whereas the emission from CDs is distinctly quenched by Pd(II). On the other hand, Glassy Carbon Electrode (GCE), which was modified by using Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles, displayed a sensitive electrochemical signal upon interaction with Pd(II). The reduction in emission intensity and peak current increase are both more pronounced for Pd-imp@CDs-Pc than for N-imp@CDs-Pc, which was synthesized in the absence of Pd(II), revealing a selective recognition ability of the Pd-imp@CDs-Pc. The emission ratio of fluorescence obtained at two wavelengths (F456/F698) diminished and peak current increased with the increase in Pd(II) concentration in the range of 2.7–90.4 µM and 5.6–390 nM, respectively. The limits of detection (LOD) for determining Pd(II) based on ratiometric fluorescence and electrochemical measurements were found to be 0.19 µM and 2.76 nM, respectively. The nanohybrid sensor exhibited also good selectivity to Pd(II) over other interfering metal ions. Fluorescence and electrochemical experimental results confirmed that the sensor based on Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles was distinctively responsive and selective for determining Pd(II) in tap water and artificial seawater samples.

head.ce:title: Ion-imprinted CDs-Pc nanohybrid sensor for ratiometric fluorescence and electrochemical detection of Pd(II)

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Methodology, Visualization, Writing − review & editing, Methodology, Visualization
Writing – original draft, Software, Supervision, Conceptualization

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Istanbul Technical University, Maslak, Istanbul 34469, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Istanbul Technical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Istanbul Technical University, 34469, Maslak, Istanbul, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report here for the first time a novel dual-fluorescence emission and electrometric sensor based on palladium ion-imprinted organosiloxane nanoparticles (Pd-imp@CDs-Pc) grafted with both carbon dots (CDs) and phthalocyanine (Pc) dye for the determination of palladium ion, Pd(II) as a target. Nanoparticles showed two emission peaks at the double excitation wavelengths of 367 and 620 nm. These peaks were assigned to emissions induced by CDs (456 nm) and Pc (698 nm). The fluorescence of Pc embedded in nanoparticles is slightly quenched, whereas the emission from CDs is distinctly quenched by Pd(II). On the other hand, Glassy Carbon Electrode (GCE), which was modified by using Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles, displayed a sensitive electrochemical signal upon interaction with Pd(II). The reduction in emission intensity and peak current increase are both more pronounced for Pd-imp@CDs-Pc than for N-imp@CDs-Pc, which was synthesized in the absence of Pd(II), revealing a selective recognition ability of the Pd-imp@CDs-Pc. The emission ratio of fluorescence obtained at two wavelengths (F456/F698) diminished and peak current increased with the increase in Pd(II) concentration in the range of 2.7–90.4 µM and 5.6–390 nM, respectively. The limits of detection (LOD) for determining Pd(II) based on ratiometric fluorescence and electrochemical measurements were found to be 0.19 µM and 2.76 nM, respectively. The nanohybrid sensor exhibited also good selectivity to Pd(II) over other interfering metal ions. Fluorescence and electrochemical experimental results confirmed that the sensor based on Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles was distinctively responsive and selective for determining Pd(II) in tap water and artificial seawater samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Palladium is a toxic and rare platinum group metal and it has a wide spectrum of applications in diverse materials such as industrial catalysis, electronics, fuel cells, and medicinal instruments owing to its remarkable physical and chemical properties [1–4]. Therefore, increasing applications of palladium in these industries can cause a not only significant amount of wastewater containing palladium but also cause pollution of the environment and serious health problems [5,6]. Palladium ions can form a complex with macromolecules such as proteins, amino acids, DNA, and RNA, and thus, it can hinder the biological process [7,8]. Therefore, it is worthy of attention to improve some sensitive and selective methods for the detection of palladium ions in biological and environmental samples.

Various conventional spectroscopic and analytical methods are reported for the detection of palladium ions including inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [9], atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [10], solid-phase microextraction–high-performance liquid chromatography (SPME–HPLC) [11] and X-ray fluorescence [12]. However, these techniques frequently require expensive equipment, complicated sample preparation or enrichment procedure, long analysis time, and professional operators. Therefore, significant endeavors have been made in past decades to develop fluorescent ion-imprinted polymers (IIPs), which are alternative to conventional methods due to their fast, reliable, simple, inexpensive, and selective properties. Since Pd(II) is found in environmental waters at trace levels, direct determination of very low concentration of Pd(II) is difficult due to matrix interferences. Therefore, a preliminary preconcentration and/or separation are necessary for sample preparation [13]. However, the preconcentration and separation methods require additional operation time and the use of large volumes of organic solvents and acids, causing the production of large volumes of waste. Since the analytical methods mentioned above require preliminary sample separation procedures such as ion-exchange and extraction, these methods are susceptible to interference with poor precision or accuracy [14]. Conversely, the DPASV used in the current work does not require complicated sample preparation or preconcentration procedure [15]. Although DPASV contains the preconcentration (accumulation) of the analyte before electrochemical measurement, it is usually performed over a short period. Additionally, the preconcentration step in stripping voltammetry analysis is carried out in-situ, which is not commonly feasible for the different analytical methods [16]. On the other hand, ratiometric fluorescence analysis gives more reliable results than single intensity-based fluorescence analysis since it is based on the ratio of dual fluorescence-emission intensities at different wavelengths. Therefore, ratiometric fluorescence detection used in the current work offers the advantages of rapid response, non-destructive methodology and real-time monitoring without professional and laborious operations. Additionally, IIPs are used as adsorbents for the removal and preconcentration of metal ions in water samples because of their high recognition capability after imprinting [17]. Therefore, fluorescence and electrochemical methods applied in the current work offer the advantages of a simple extraction and preconcentration process using novel Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles for the selective determination of Pd(II).

Recently, Kandaz et al. described a method for the determination of Pd(II) based on the thio-substituted phthalocyanine derivative[18]. They analyzed the decrease in fluorescence of the phthalocyanine molecule in the presence of a Pd(II) ion. Lin et al. developed a fluorophore for ratiometric sensing of Pd(II) based on a Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [19]. Koide et al. introduced a strategy to determine Pd(II) by an enzyme-catalyzed reaction utilizing a Pd(II) or Pd(0) which switches a molecule from strong emission of fluorescence to a non-fluorescent molecule [20]. In their articles, Peng et al.[21] and Liu et al.[22] reviewed the previous studies based on the determination of Pd(II) ion by fluorescence spectroscopy. Bai et al. developed a voltammetric sensing system utilizing a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with an ion-imprinted polymer (IIP) and graphene for the detection of Pd(II) ion in complex matrices. In this system, IIP maintained the recognition of Pd(II) by acting as a pre-concentrator, whereas graphene improves the surface area of the electrode [23]. Bojdi et al. reported on the fabrication of an electrode based on the use of IIP nanoparticles for differential pulse voltammetry detection of Pd(II) in the existence of interfering ions [24].

Ion-imprinting is the technique for forming specific recognition sites in a polymer matrix using a ligand-metal interaction between the functional molecule and metal ions [25,26]. The ion-imprinted polymers (IIPs) are synthesized by using with target metal ion, a functional monomer that is capable of chelating to the target ion, cross-linker, and initiator. End of the polymerization process, the target ion is extracted from the polymer matrix, thus three-dimensional IIPs are obtained with specific recognition cavities suitable for the shape and size of target metal ions [27]. The remarkable selectivity of IIPs is based on two factors; dimensions of created cavities in a polymer network and affinity of the functional monomer to metal ion [28]. In this way, functional monomers located in a certain arrangement around the target metal ion ensure that the binding cavities match the size and dimensional analogs of the ion like a key and lock model [29].

Sensors based on IIPs, which are combined with fluorescent analysis and specific recognition capability, have gained increasing attention in past decades owing to their sensitivity, high selectivity, low cost, ease of production, and mechanical and chemical stabilities [30]. However, these sensors are generally impressed by the complex environmental conditions of samples, inhomogeneous distribution of polymers, and instrument performance, which may avoid accurate quantitative determination of target ions and molecules [31,32]. Ratiometric sensors incorporating two kinds of fluorophores, one as reference dye and another one as target-sensitive dye, can simultaneously measure and produce two fluorescence spectra [33,34]. The ratiometric measurement that includes calculating the intensity ratios of the two fluorescent spectra depending on the target molecule concentration is designed to prevent both the unfavorable effects of conventional IIPs sensors and false-positive results [35,36]. On the other hand, electrochemical determination of Pd(II) by electrodes modified with IIPs has received increasing attention [37]. Moreover, differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) by using modified electrodes is the most remarkable electrochemical technique for the detection of trace metals due to its extreme sensitivity [23].

In this study, ion-imprinted organosiloxane nanoparticles grafted with CDs and Pc were synthesized for the determination of Pd(II) based on ratiometric fluorescence and electrochemical measurements. CDs and Pc were used for preparing an ion-imprinted polymer, which was fabricated as a nanohybrid sensor, was utilized both for ratiometric fluorescence and electrochemical detection of the Pd(II). The grafting of IIP with CDs and Pc imparted high surface area and template selectivity to the sensor. The imprinted nanohybrid probe exhibits ratiometric fluorescence emissions centered at 456 and 698 nm upon applying a dual excitation wavelength of 360 nm and 620 nm, respectively. The green-emitting Pc embedded in the organosiloxane core radiates reference signals at 698 nm for supplying established adjustments to prevent environmental effects. At the same time, blue-emitting CDs immobilized in imprinted organosiloxane shell at 456 nm are quenched by Pd(II), creating a ratiometric fluorescent sensor. Moreover, a combination of IIPs and an electrochemical method was also utilized to fabricate a sensitive ion-imprinted sensor for the determination of Pd(II). The electrochemical performance of a developed IIP sensor, which was fabricated by modification of electrode with Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles, was investigated by using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and DPASV methods at room temperature.

In most of the ratiometric fluorescence sensors, dye as a probe is usually bounded to nanoparticles physically which usually affects homogeneous distribution and the stability of sensor response [38,39]. Therefore, one of the goals of this work was to obtain chemically bonded dye (Pc) on nanoparticles (CDs). For this purpose, Pc was functionalized with organosilane, ICPTS and was incorporated on CDs through covalent bonding via the formation of a polysiloxane network to obtain a stable ratiometric fluorescence response. The reason for choosing the Pc, which exhibits emission at a longer wavelength (698 nm), is to avoid fluorescence quenching due to the inner filter effect caused by heavy metal ions, leading to a high interference effect that reduces the selective sensing of Pd(II). To the best of our knowledge, no ratiometric fluorescence and electrometric sensor based on Pd(II)-imprinted CDs-Pc sensor has been reported so far for the detection of Pd(II), and also there is no IIPs-based ratiometric study for the determination of Pd (II). Since the ratiometric measurement calculates the intensity ratios of the two fluorescence spectra depending on the analyte concentration, it avoids the inconvenient effects of conventional IIPs sensors in water samples because of poor selectivity and matrix implications. The proposed sensor developed in this work has significance in the routine analysis as a practical sensor to determine trace amounts of Pd(II) in water samples with satisfactory results.

The novelty of this study lies in the synthesis of zinc phthalocyanine containing four peripheral 1-hydroxyhexan-3-ylthio groups and functionalization with an organosilane, which enables the incorporation of Pc onto CDs nanoparticles by covalent bonding via the formation of nanopolymer network, and hence nanohybrid sensor system based on Pd(II)-imprinted polysiloxane nanoparticles grafted with CDs and Pc dye was developed for ratiometric fluorescence and electrochemical detection of Pd(II). Owing to guaranteed ion imprinting of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles, excellent selectivity and sensitivity was achieved for the determination of Pd(II) in the presence of interfering metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental section
Reagents and chemicals
ce:section.ce:para: 3-(2-Aminoethylamino)propyl-dimethoxymethylsilane (APDMS), Palladium(II) chloride (PdCl2), 3-sulfanyl-1-hexanol(SH), 2,3-dicyanonitrobenzene(DNB), 1,8-Diazabicyclo [5.4.0]undec-7-ene (DBU) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Darmstadt, Germany). Citric acid (CA), (3-Aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), and Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) were provided from Alfa Aesar (Lancaster, UK). Zinc acetate Zn(OAc)2, potassium carbonate (K2CO3), silver nitrate (AgNO3), mercury nitrate monohydrate (Hg(NO3)2·H2O), lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2), strontium nitrate (Sr(NO3)2), magnesium nitrate hexahydrate (Mg(NO3)2.6 H2O), cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate (Cd(NO3)2.4 H2O), nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2.6 H2O), barium nitrate (Ba(NO3)2), iron nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3.9 H2O) were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Silica gel (Merck grade 60 and Sephadex) used for chromatography was purchased from Aldrich. All chemicals were used as received without further treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Apparatus
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence measurements were performed by using a Varian Cary-Eclipse Luminesce spectrometer using a quartz cell (1.0 cm × 1.0 cm). Fluorescence spectra were obtained with varying excitation wavelengths. The slit widths of excitation and emission were adjusted to 5 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the samples were recorded by using an Agilent Cary 630 FTIR spectrometer. VWR pH-meter 730 P was utilized for measurements of the pH of the solution. Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM) images were acquired on LEO Supra VP-55 microscope for the characterization of the shape and surface morphology of nanoparticles. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of samples were obtained by using JEOL JEM-2100 (UHR) instrument for elucidation of the size and structure of nanoparticles. Preparation of samples for TEM was carried out by dropping 5 μL of sample solution onto a carbon-coated copper grid and drying at ambient temperature. The dynamic light scattering technique (DLS) was operated for the size distribution of the nanoparticles by using a Malvern Zeta-sizer Nano-ZS90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd, UK) using a rectangular quartz cell. 1H and 13C NMR spectra were recorded on Agilent VNMRS 500 MHz spectrometer instruments. Versastat 3 (Princeton Applied Research, Oak Ridge, TN) was used as a potentiostat/galvanostat for electrochemical measurements. A glassy carbon microelectrode, Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl), and a platinum wire were employed as working, reference, and counter electrodes, respectively in a voltammetric cell. Before each electrochemical measurement, the nitrogen gas was purged through the electrolyte for 3 min to eliminate the oxygen, and measurements were carried out under a nitrogen atmosphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs, Pc, and Pc-ICPTS precursor
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were prepared using anhydrous citric acid and APDMS as an organofunctional silane molecule applying the following method; 10 mL APDMS was damped into a 100 mL three-necked flask, and nitrogen was used for degassing the system for 10 min. After heating the system to 240 0C, then 0.5 g of anhydrous citric acid was put into the flask under stirring. The mixture was stirred for 1 min at the high temperature of the system. After the system was cooled down to the ambient temperature, the product was purified by petroleum ether ( Fig. 1). As-prepared CDs were characterized by UV–vis (Fig. S1) and fluorescence measurements (Fig.S2 and Fig.S3).

Pc containing zinc metal (Pc) synthesized according to our previous study [40]. Before the synthesis of Pc, the synthesis of 3-(1-hydroxyhexan-3-ylthio)phthalonitrile (HTPN) was carried out by the following procedure. After dissolving 12.0 mmol of SH in 10.0 mL of DMF, 17.0 mmol of K2CO3 was put into the solution, and then 12.0 mmol of DNB dissolved in 10.0 mL of DMF was added into the first solution by dropwise at 40 0C under N2 atmosphere. The solution mixture was stirred efficiently under these conditions for one day. After that, the mixture cooled down to ambient temperature and was damped into 300 mL of ice water to precipitate the product. For removing the impurities, the precipitated product was dissolved in chloroform and washed with 2.5% NaHCO3 and anhydrous Na2SO4. After evaporation of chloroform, HTPN was obtained by subsequently implementing the silica gel column chromatography (CHCl3: MeOH (95: 5) eluent). Pc was synthesized by the reaction of 600 mg of HTPN with 120 μL of DBU by the way of heating to 170 0C under an N2 atmosphere. After that, the reaction mixture was stirred for 30 min and then 96 mg of anhydrous Zn(OAc)2 was added to the solution. The mixture was heated and stirred for 8 h more and then the product, Pc was precipitated and washed with hot heptane, a mixture of isopropanol-water and acetonitrile, respectively. In the end, Pc was purified by applying the two silica gel column chromatography (THF: MeOH (50:1 v/v) eluent), (CHCl3: MeOH (20:1 v/v) eluent) and washing with hexane and diethyl ether ( Fig. 2). 1H and 13C NMR spectra of Pc in DMSO-d6 were given in Fig S4. 1H NMR (DMSO-d6) δ, ppm: 9.23(d, br, 1 H, Phenyl H4), 9.12 (m, br, 1 H, Phenyl H5), 8.16 (d, br, 1 H, Phenyl H6), 4.72 (s, br, 1 H, -CH2-OH), 3.96 (t, br, 2 H, -CH2-OH), 3.82 (m, br, 1 H, CH2CH-S), 3.37 (DMSO), 2.03–1.86 (m, br, 2 H, CH2CH2CH-S), 1.75–1.64 (q, br, 2 H, CH2CH2CH-S), 1.52–1.49 (m, br, 2 H, CH2CH2CH3), 1.08 (t, br, 3 H, CH3). 13C NMR(DMSO-d6) δ, ppm: 151.08 (S-Ar-C3), 136.29 (Ar- C5), 130.05 (Ar-C4), 129.34 (Ar-C6), 128.62 (Ar-C1), 127.32 (Ar-C2), 121.73 (Ar-CN), 58.76 (-CH2-OH), 40.87 (DMSO), 38.22 (CH2CH2-OH), 37.19 (CH-S), 30.87 (CH2CH2CH3), 29.53 (CH2CH2CH3), 14.62 (CH3).

Pc-ICPTS was produced by the reaction between Pc and ICPTS. The procedure for the production of Pc-ICPTS was as follows: 1.0 mmol of Pc was first dissolved in 4 mL of THF and then 4.0 mmol of ICPTS was put into solution drop by drop. The reaction was carried out at 60 0C for 24 h under a nitrogen atmosphere. After removing THF by evaporation, the product was obtained by flash column chromatography (silica gel, CHCl3/CH3OH = 15/1, v/v), providing (68.7%) green-colored compound (Fig. 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-imp and Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the sol-gel method, nanoparticles were synthesized as follows; 50 mL of water and 40 mL of ethanol were mixed in a round-bottom flask with a capacity of 500 mL. After the addition of 200 μL of CDs and 200 μL of ammonia solution (25%) into the solution under stirring, 40 mL of ethanol containing 100 μL of TEOS were injected, and then the final mixture was stirred for 6 h. In a separate vial, 4.0 mL of Pc-ICPTS solution, 80 μL of APTES, and 2.0 mL of Pd(II) solution (10−3 M in methanol) were dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol and added to the final mixture, and then stirred for one hour. Afterward, 3.2 mL of CTAB solution (0.2 M), 0.4 mL of NaOH solution (0.2 M), and 200 μL of TEOS were injected into the resultant mixture and stirred for 24 h more at a temperature of 30 0C. The final product containing Pd(II) was obtained by centrifugation and washed with water and ethanol, respectively two times. Washing with 0.1 M HCl was carried out to remove Pd (II) ion and, then the obtained Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles were dried under vacuum and stored at 4 0C ( Fig. 3). Non-imprinted nanoparticles (N-imp@CDs-Pc) were obtained by applying same procedure without using Pd(II) ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GCE modified with N-imp and Pd-imp@CDs-Pc
ce:section.ce:para: Before the application process of modification, the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was cleaned with alumina slurry (1.0, 0.3, and 0.05 µm, respectively) to obtain an electrode with a shiny surface. After rinsing with pure water, the electrode was sonicated for 3 min in ethanol and allowed to dry in the air. The solution containing Pd-imp@CDs-Pc was obtained by dispersing 1.0 mg of nanoparticles in 1.0 mL of ethanol. Finally, the GCE electrode modified with Pd-imp@CDs-Pc (Pd-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE) was fabricated by dropping 10 μL of solution of nanoparticles onto the GCE surface and then drying under a vacuum. A similar modification procedure was applied to prepare a non-imprinted electrode (N-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE) by using a solution of N-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical procedure
ce:section.ce:para: Since palladium(II) chloride (PdCl2) is insoluble in water, soluble salt of Pd(II), which is sodium tetrachloropalladate (Na2PdCl4), was prepared simply by reacting PdCl2 with the appropriate sodium chloride in methanol solution at 60 0C. The stock solution of Pd(II) with a concentration of 1.0 × 10−3 M obtained in methanol was used to prepare the standard solution with buffer solution by further dilution.

For fluorescence measurements, 200 μL of stock solution of N-imp or Pd-imp@CDs-Pc (1.0 mg/mL) and Pd (II) containing standard or sample solution was placed in a quartz cuvette and then the volume was diluted to 2.0 mL with 20 mM citrate buffer (pH 3.0) solution. The ratios of fluorescence intensities at 456 and 698 nm (F456/F698) were recorded by applying excitation wavelengths of 367 and 620 nm, respectively. For electrochemical measurements, the cell containing 10.0 mL of 20 mM acetate buffer (pH 3.6), 0.1 M of KCl, and a specified concentration of Pd(II) solution was prepared and Pd-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE electrode was immersed into the cell and then Pd(II) was accumulated for 6 min with stirring under open-circuit conditions. After a waiting time of 10 s, differential pulse anodic stripping voltammetry (DPASV) was applied to re-oxidize the accumulated Pd(0) without stirring the solution with the scan range potential of 0.35–1.0 V; step height of 5 mV, step time of 100 ms, the pulse of 0.1 V for 50 ms and a scan rate of 50 mV.s−1. The modified electrode was regenerated for the subsequent run by desorption of the accumulated species in 0.1 M of HCl solution for 10 min by using an ultrasonic bath. Repeating three times measurements, the average height of three peak currents was recorded. Similar experimental conditions were also applied for the voltammetric investigation on N-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE and bare GCE electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Spectral characterization of N-imp and Pd-imp@CDs-Pc
ce:section.ce:para: As seen from the FT-IR spectrum of Pc ( Fig. 4, spectrum a), 2922 and 2860 cm−1 peaks show scissoring vibrations of CH2-O; 1598 indicates double bonds (CC), 1482, 1434, 1382, and 1297 cm−1 display bending bands of CO-H, 1093 and 1035 cm−1 show wagging vibrations of CH2-O. The peak at 2269 cm−1, which is indicative of the isocyanate group(–NCO) of ICPTS (Fig. 4, spectrum b), disappeared but the peak at 1670 cm−1 belonging to the amide group appeared in the spectrum of Pc-ICPTS (Fig. 4, spectrum c), indicating the completion of reaction between ICPTS and Pc.

FT-IR spectra of CDs and Pd-imp@CDs-Pc were also obtained and compared (Fig. 4, spectra d and e). The peaks at 2921 cm−1 and 2832 cm−1 originate from the –CH3 and –CH2– vibrations of CDs functionalized by the ethoxy group, respectively, which disappeared in the Pd-imp@CDs-Pc spectrum. The peak at 1661 cm−1 was associated with CC stretching vibrations. A sharp absorption band at 1637 cm−1 arises due to the vibrational bending of N-H. The peak at 1592 cm−1 is assigned to the deformation vibration of –C-N, 1452 cm−1 belongs to the symmetrical deformation vibration of –CH3, 1192 cm−1 can be identified as C-O stretching, 1145 cm−1 is assigned to Si–O–Si asymmetric stretching that was not seen in the spectrum of CDs (Fig. 4, spectrum d), the intense peak at 1045 cm−1 is assigned to the Si–O–C. The peak at about 799 cm−1 also provides knowledge on the presence of bending vibration of the Si–O group. The bands explained in all over the FTIR spectra prove that the organosiloxane polymer structure was formed on the surface of CDs with Pc.

The surface morphology and size of N-imp and Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles were analyzed by SEM measurements ( Fig. 5). SEM view of N-imp@CDs-Pc revealed that nanoparticles have spherical morphology with uniform size and a corrugated surface (Fig. 5A).

Moreover mean size of N-imp@CDs-Pc was substantially identical with Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles (Fig. 5B), revealing that Pd(II) recognition ability of imprinted nanoparticles was due to the creation of specific pores, not discrimination in surface morphology among CDs-Pc nanoparticles.

The shape and size distribution of the CDs, N-imp and Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles are picturized in Fig. S5 and Fig. 6. As seen from the TEM image, CDs nanoparticles demonstrate uniform size and distribution (Fig. S5). The TEM image of N-imp@CDs-Pc disclosed that organosiloxane nanoparticles were uniformly distributed without remarkable aggregation (Fig. 6A), whereas Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles seemed to be aggregated with various interfaces among them because of the accumulation of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc due to the concentration used for sample preparation in the aqueous solution.(Fig. 6B).
The measurements of DLS display that the average sizes of CDs, N-imp and Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles are about 10.4, 25 and 30 nm, respectively (Fig. S6, Figs. 6C and 6D).

Since the same concentrations of CDs, Pc, and siloxane precursors were used to obtain Pd-imp@CDs-Pc and N-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles, the formation of polysiloxane network surrounding the surface of CDs creates a bigger dimension in the presence of Pd(II). The removal of Pd(II) from the network keeps the dimension of imprinted CDs-Pc same but creates a more porous structure. Since these cavities do not exist in the structure of N-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles, we may define a bigger surface area for imprinted polymer in comparison with non-imprinted polymer. Therefore, the bigger dimension of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc compared with N-imp@CDs-Pc may also indicate the formation of imprinted cavities on the surface of CDs-Pc nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and electrochemical characterization of N-imp and Pd-imp@CDs-Pc
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectra of as-prepared Pd-imp@CDs-Pc were recorded applying different excitation wavelengths with a 10 nm increment ( Fig. 7A). Both maximum intensity and maximum wavelength upon excitation wavelength were obtained from fluorescence spectra of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc and were plotted against applied excitation wavelengths (Fig. 7B).

As seen from Fig. 7B, emission intensity depending on the excitation wavelength increases up to the wavelength of 360 nm and, then decreases as the excitation wavelength is further enlarged. Meanwhile, the maximum wavelength shifts to the red region upon an increase in excitation wavelength.

On the other hand, the maximum intensity was observed at 462 nm when the excitation wavelength was set at 340 nm, whereas it was observed at 466 nm when excited at 400 nm. As seen from Fig. 7B, the range of change in maximum wavelength seems to be low, showing that there existed narrow distribution of the particle sizes and Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles produced are almost similar dimensions.

The electrochemical characterization and analytical performance of N-imp and Pd-imp@CDs-Pc /GCE were carried out in a 20 mM acetate buffer (pH 3.6) solution containing 0.1 M KCl as a supporting electrolyte by using CV and DPASV methods at room temperature. The analytical performance of the electrodes was investigated in several steps. In the first step, the modified electrode was immersed in buffer solution with different Pd(II) concentrations for 5 min to accumulate the recognition cavities of imprinted polymer with Pd(II) ions. The electrode was washed with pure water and then dipped into an electrochemical cell containing 0.1 M HCl solution, and Pd(II) ions were reduced to Pd(0) by applying E = -1.0V vs Ag/AgCl for 20 s. After an equilibrium time of 10 s, a DPASV run was applied to re-oxidize the accumulated Pd(0) without stirring the solution.
CVs as shown in Fig. 8 characterized the electrochemical behavior of different electrodes in the presence and absence of 1.0 × 10−7 M Pd (II) in buffer solution with 0.1 M KCl.

As seen in Fig. 8, no peak was observed for bare GCE in the absence of Pd(II) ion. When bare GCE is coated with Pd-imp@CDs-Pc, an anodic peak at 0.326 V and a cathodic peak at 0.22 V versus Ag/AgCl were observed. These peaks might belong to oxidation and reduction of the Pc ring, respectively [41]. With the addition of Pd(II) ions into the electrolyte, the peaks for the Pc ring disappeared and new peaks were observed at 0.1 V and − 0.35 V, belonging to the reduction and oxidation of Pd(II), respectively [42].

For comparison, the CVs of N-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE were taken in buffer with and without 1.0 × 10−7 M Pd(II). As seen from the inset of Fig. 8, the CVs were almost the same in the presence (curve d) and absence (curve e) of Pd(II) on N-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE and all the oxidation and reduction peaks of Pd(II) disappeared compare to the imprinted electrode. Because the non-conducting N-imp layer covered the surface and blocked the electron transfer to the electrode. This result revealed that Pd-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE was to be possessed various specific binding sites on the nanosensor layer that existed for selective detection of Pd(II) ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on performance of the nanohybrid sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
Since the chemical structures of Pd-containing compounds (e.g.,
P d C
l
2
,
P d C
l
3
−
and
P d C
l
4
2 −
) exist in solution depending on pH, their interaction capability with the recognition cavities in Pd-imp@CDs-Pc is related to the pH of the sample solution. Therefore, the pH effect on the fluorescence signal of the nanohybrid sensor in the absence and presence of Pd(II) should be illuminated.

Fluorescence spectra of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc upon excitation at both 367 nm and 620 nm were obtained in the absence and presence of Pd(II) ion ( Fig. 9A and Fig. 9B). Transformation of monovalent
P d C
l
3
−
anions to divalent
P d C
l
4
2 −
anions has occurred as pH increased from 2.0 to 4.0 and the surface charge of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc remains positively charged due to the existence of amino-functional silane, APTES [43]. As such, electrostatic interaction between the Pd(II) compound and the grafted Pd-imp@CDs-Pc becomes strong in the range of pH 2 ∼ 4. As seen from the inset of Fig. 9A, a continuous decrease in emission at 456 nm, which is due to the core CDs, was observed upon an increase in pH, whereas an increase in emission intensity at 698 nm, which is due to the Pc molecule, was noted upon altering the pH from 2.0 to 4.0. Then the intensity was diminished upon increment in pH because of the decline in conjugation due to the protonation of nitrogen atoms in the Pc ring. As a consequence, the difference in the degree of quenching has increased with the increase of pH both in the absence and the presence of Pd (II). Emission ratio values, F456/F698 upon excitation at both 367 nm and 620 nm obtained from each spectrum of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc were also represented as a function of pH (Fig. 9C). As seen, the maximum ratio of quenching was observed at pH 2.0 and then diminished as the pH ascended continually from 2 to 4. Eventually, the most obvious Pd(II) species are negatively charged like
P d C
l
3
−
and
P d C
l
4
2 −
, and the Pd-imp@CDs-Pc has positive charged. Although the highest decrease in ratio was observed at pH 2.0, the pH of the solution was chosen as 3.0 for all the following fluorescence studies since high acid conditions could give damage the nanoparticles [5].

The stability of the Pd-imp@CDs-Pc sensor was elucidated by measuring the fluorescence intensities at the maximum emission peaks every 5 min in the absence of a Pd(II) ion. The fluorescence emissions at both 356 and 698 nm from the nanohybrid sensor were fairly consistent within 30 min, showing that the nanoparticles were dispersed in solution without pronounced accumulation (Fig S7). The time response of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc to Pd(II) was optimized. For this purpose, the solution of the nanohybrid sensor (0.1 mg/mL) was prepared in the presence of 5.0 × 10−5 M Pd(II) in buffer at pH 3.0. The emission of fluorescence in the time interval of 1–30 min was monitored and the data showed that the responses of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc decreased exponentially within 5 min and then stayed nearly stable (Fig. S8). Therefore, the optimal time of response was chosen as 5 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric performance of the nanohybrid sensor
ce:section.ce:para: As seen in Fig. 10 (A and B), the emission at 456 nm belonging to the fluorescence response of CDs in N-imp@CDs-Pc (Fig. 10A) was quenched less than that of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc (Fig. 10B) upon addition of Pd(II), whereas quenching efficiency of Pc at 698 nm in both nanohybrid sensors towards Pd(II) was almost same. This shows that the interaction of Pd(II) with CDs in the sensor is more predominant than that of Pc located inside the sensor and also revealed that the specific binding sides for recognition of Pd(II) significantly enhanced the quenching efficiency of CDs in the nanohybrid sensor. The outcomes pointed out that the calibration graphs of the N-imp and Pd-imp@CDs-Pc exhibited linearity correlation in the scale of Pd(II) concentration (Fig. 10C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electroanalytical performance of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of the electrodes was revealed by incubating the N-imp and Pd-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE in solutions of Pd(II) with concentration increment and ensuing DPASV scanning after each incubation process. Fig. 11 shows that the current intensity of the peak for Pd-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE is stronger than that for N-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE (Fig. 11, inset), indicating the creation of functionalized cavities for the binding of Pd(II).

Fig. 11B shows the change in peak currents of DPASV versus various concentrations of Pd(II) on N-imp and Pd-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE. As can be seen from Fig. 11B, both of the electrodes show a similar way of acting in the concentration interval investigated, whereas the maximum peak height (∆IP) on N-imp@CD-Pc/GCE is much lower than that of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE. This could be illuminated by creating specific recognition cavities in the imprinted polymer network after the removal of Pd(II), leading to a more porous structure compared to the non-imprinted polymer. The porosity enhances the diffusion and binding of Pd(II) into the film of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE, however, the film of N-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE acts as a barrier between the solution and film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Figures of merit for the determination of Pd(II)
ce:section.ce:para:
The ratiometric fluorescence and electrochemical sensor evolved in this study are talented for identifying Pd(II) selectively and sensitively based on ratiometric emission and electrochemical current measurements. To confirm this assertion, several concentrations of Pd(II) were examined using optimum conditions. The calibration equations
F

)
for fluorescence measurements and

for electrochemical measurements were obtained from the relevant curves. Based on the description of 3Sb/m, where Sb symbolizes the deviation from the blank sample and m is the slope of the calibration equation, the limit of detection (LOD) values of 0.19 µM and 2.76 nM were achieved for the determination of Pd(II) using ratiometric fluorescence and electrochemical measurements, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoprobe for detecting Pd(II)
ce:section.ce:para: A crucial subject in the progression of any sensor is selectivity. The specific selectivity of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc to Pd(II) over other interfering metal ions is critical for a sensor upon application in environmental conditions. The responses of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc to varied species such as Ba2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Sr2+, Ag+, Co2+, Fe2+ (all at 0.1 mM) were monitored in the absence and the presence of 0.1 mM Pd(II) ion to examine the selectivity of the fabricated sensor systems. In addition, the selectivity of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc for the analytes was also investigated.

Fig. 12 A displays the selectivity of ratiometric fluorescence signals of the nanoprobe in the existence of the potentially interfering metal ions. As shown in Fig. 12A, besides the effect of Pd(II), the impact of others on the alteration emission ratio was also examined under similar circumstances. Upon addition of an equivalent concentration of metal ions into the solution of the fluorescent sensor, only Pd(II) seriously quenched the emission of the sensor, diminishing the ratio, F456/F698 up to 59%, conversely, quenching of fluorescence ratio was not observed in the presence of other interfering metal ions. On the other hand, following the addition of Pd(II) to overall mixtures including interference metal ions, the ratio of F456/F698 has diminished apparently and reached the eventual ratio of less than 8.0%, revealing that the presence of these metal ions has a minor interfering effect on the convenient selectivity of the dual-emission nanosensor upon exposing to Pd(II) ions. This indicates that the improvement of selectivity related to the usage of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc is due to the high-affinity interaction of Pd(II) with recognition cavities inside the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE electrode
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, we verified the selectivity by achieving two groups of electrochemical measurements using modified electrodes and then the selectivity of electrodes towards other metal ion species was examined. In this experiment, electrodes coated with N-imp and Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles were immersed into a solution containing 4.6×10−8 M of Pd(II) and 25-fold excess of the interfering metal ions for 5 min and, then DPASV run was applied. The difference in peak current, ΔI, (ΔI = I0-Ic, where I0 and Ic are the current response of the proposed sensor accumulated in a solution containing Pd(II) and interference metal ions, respectively) was depicted in Fig. 12B. As shown in Fig. 12B, the existence of interfering metal ions caused a minimal difference in current due to their nominal affinity with Pd-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE, while an evident current change was obtained upon addition of an equivalent amount of Pd(II) in the identical conditions. The results revealed that electrode surface coated with Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles could discriminate between Pd(II) and interference metal ions in solution. On the other hand, the change in current in the presence of Pd(II) and interference metal ions by using the N-imp and Pd-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE electrodes were also compared to elucidate the selectivity. As seen from Fig. 12B, the current of N-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE was not affected by sequential additions of both Pd(II) and interference metal ions, revealing that the enhanced current of the Pd-imp@CDs-Pc modified electrode was selective for Pd(II) recognition.

The imprinted factor (f), which is the ratio of current intensities of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc (ΔIMIP) to N-imp@CDs-Pc (ΔINIP), was also calculated (Table S1). The higher f-value for Pd(II) proves that the designed sensor is more responsive to the target ion compared to the interfering ions [44]. Moreover, when the f-value of a sensor approaches 1.0, it means less imprinting behavior of the sensor and the sensing process is unaffected by the interfering metal ions.

The selective characters of N-imp and Pd-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE electrodes were also evaluated by the selectivity coefficient (k) and relative selectivity coefficient (k ´ ), and the results were shown in Table S1. The higher values of selectivity coefficients of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE (kMIP) were obtained compared with N-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE (kNIP) suggesting that electrode selectivity towards Pd(II) was greater than that of interference metal ions [45]. The relative selectivity coefficient values acquired for the interfering ions are higher than 1.0 means that specific binding sites on the electrode surface recognize Pd(II) selectively based on the dimension and pattern of cavities localized in structure [46]. The high value of the relative selectivity coefficient of the Pd-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE electrode also indicated that the imprinted electrode is more selective to Pd(II) than interference metal ions.

The performance of the fabricated sensor based on ratiometric fluorescence and electrochemical detection of Pd(II) was compared with some of the previously studied sensors ( Table 1). It should be mentioned that selective sensing of Pd(II) plays an important role in the usage of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor developed in this study although the normal LOD value was obtained. Moreover, a low LOD value for the determination of Pd(II) was achieved by using the modified electrode, offering the advantage of applicability for the determination of Pd(II) concentration.

One of the factors that lead to low LOD value in electrochemical measurements is DPASV which is a mostly used electrochemical method for the determination of trace heavy metal ions. In the DPASV method, firstly, the metal ion is reduced and preconcentrated on the working electrode by applying a constant potential, and then the preconcentrated metal ions are stripped (removed) from the electrode by sweeping the potential in the anodic direction by differential pulse voltammetry [52]. Another factor leading to remarkably low detection limits is the modification of the electrodes with nanoparticles [53]. The low detection limit of this study might be attributed to the accumulation of the Pd(II) on the surface of the working electrode coated with Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles where Pd(II) was captured by functional groups, APTES located inside specific cavities [54]. As a result, the usage of CDs-Pc with high specific surface area and Pd-imp@CDs-Pc with specific recognition cavities to modify the glassy carbon electrode, and the application of DPASV as a measurement method for the determination of Pd(II) resulted in an increase in sensitivity and a decrease in detection limit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The imprinted recognition mechanism of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The imprinted recognition mechanism of the imprinted polymer toward Pd(II) could be explained from the aspect of the orientation of functional monomers on nanosensor and the creation of a specific cavity that has a suitable size for the adsorption of Pd(II) [55]. The functional monomer, APTES, which contains an amine group, is charged positively at low pH values and interacts with Pd(II) since Pd(II) is in divalent anion form with a structure of
P d C
l
4
2 −
( Scheme 1). The imprinting technique provides the suitable location of APTES molecules for capturing one Pd(II) as a pair. The memory of pairing of APTES within the nanoparticle was encoded in the primary sequence of the main monomer, APTES within the Pd-imp@CDs-Pc by synthesizing network while APTES molecules were paired before polymerization. The N-imp@CDs-Pc, where APTES molecules were randomly distributed, exhibited frustration in forming pairs, whereas such frustration was completely declined in the Pd-imp@CDs-Pc, delivering the memory of pair formation. When the performances of the Pd-imp@CDs-Pc and N-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles as fluorescence probes or modifiers on GCEs were compared, distinctly quenching of fluorescence and a significant increase in the current response of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc verified that IIP modifier contained much more Pd (II) absorbing sites than NIP modifier. According to the selectivity study of Pd-imp@CDs-Pc in this work, the other interfering metal ions exhibited both low degree of change in quenching and current in the ratiometric fluorescence the electrochemical methods, respectively, revealing the low binding ability of metal ions to nanosensor. If recognition were based on the only size effect, then there would be a certain interference effect of the metal ions with almost similar sizes to Pd(II). This shows that the orientation of functional monomers that interact with Pd(II) is more pronounced than the size effect for recognition, showing that the suitable located APTES molecules capture the Pd(II) upon specified interaction and also indicating that Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles are inclined to bind Pd(II) with more effective functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The response and detection mechanism of sensor’
ce:section.ce:para:
As a heavy- and (inner-) transition-metal ion, Pd(II) is a typical fluorescence quencher. Fluorescence quenching is caused by complex interactions such as intramolecular charge transfer, paramagnetic influences and heavy atom effects [21]. There are usually two fluorescence quenching mechanisms: dynamic (collisional) quenching and static (complex formation) quenching. The dynamic quenching mechanism results from diffusive collisions between the fluorophore and quencher during the lifetime of the excited state, whereas the static quenching occurs as a result of the formation of a non-fluorescent complex between fluorophore and quencher [56]. the fluorescence quenching was analyzed using the Stern–Volmer (S-V) equation:
(1)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the fluorophore in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively. [Q] is quencher concentration and KSV is the S-V quenching constant. The S-V plot was obtained for the fluorescence quenching of CDs and Pc in Pd-imp@CDs-Pc with increasing Pd (II) concentration (Fig. S9). As seen in Fig. S9, the S-V plot for CDs showed a positive deviation from linearity (upward-curved), possibly suggesting the simultaneous presence of both dynamic and static mechanisms [57]. Moreover, S-V plot displayed a linear response at low (below 90 µM) concentration of Pd(II) and quenching constant (KSV) was found to be 1.24 × 104 mol−1dm3 from the slope of the graph. On the other hand, the S-V plot for Pc emission (698 nm) showed a parabolic shape exhibiting saturation at high (above 120 µM) concentration of Pd(II). The reason for fluorescence quenching of nanoparticle emission at longer wavelength might be due to the interaction of Pd(II) with Sulphur-donor atoms just attached to Pc core ( Scheme 1)[18], revealing that metal coordination-induced fluorescence quenching is possible mechanism for detecting Pd(II) at longer emission of sensor [58]. On the other hand, if the sensing mechanism is based on aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching, then quenching effect would be the same for both CDs and Pc [59]. But results showed that the emission peak of Pc was slightly quenched, whereas the fluorescence signal of the emission peak of CDs was distinctly quenched by the Pd (II), indicating that Pd(II)–induced aggregation has less effect on fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery of the sensor on real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of developed Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles-based ratiometric fluorescence and electrometric sensors was assessed by applying it for detection of Pd(II) in tap and artificial seawater samples spiked with a known amount of standard solutions.

For ratiometric fluorescence measurements, two different concentrations (5.0 and 10.0 μM) of Pd(II) were added to the water samples. Each added amount of Pd(II) was examined by repeating three times, and the averages were demonstrated as relative standard deviation (RSD). As indicated in Table 2, convincing recoveries were ensured in the interval of 104–109%, with RSDs of 2.9–7.3%, indicating that the obtained Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanosensor was feasible for the precise detection of Pd(II) in a complex medium such as artificial seawater or tap water samples.

The Pd-imp@CDs-Pc/GCE electrode was also tested for electrochemical analysis of Pd(II) in tap water and artificial seawater samples to estimate the feasible usage of the fabricated sensor. Two different concentrations (50 and 100 nM) of Pd(II) were added to the water samples for electrochemical analysis and the procedure was the same as stated in the experimental section. As seen in Table 2, the recoveries are between 98.5% and 102.4%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) lies in the range from 0.68% to 1.06% claiming that the quantitative determination of Pd(II) in the water samples might be precisely done by the recommended electrochemical method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: An effectual approach for contriving the ratiometric fluorescent and electrometric sensor based on ion-imprinted organosiloxane nanoparticles for the determination of Pd(II) has been described. In the designed structure of the sensor, CDs were attached to the core while Pc molecules were grafted on the outer shell of organosiloxane construction. Fluorescence emission at 698 nm from Pc molecules operates as a reference signal while the emission at 456 nm of CDs in an imprinted silica shell, which served as a pioneer marker for successive detection, is selectively quenched by Pd(II) even in the existence of interfering metal ions, showing the improvement of selectivity and accuracy of the ratiometric fluorescent sensor. Electrochemical results revealed that the GCE electrode modified with Pd-imp@CDs-Pc nanoparticles exhibited a sensitive electrochemical signal upon interaction with Pd(II) since the high peak current was obtained in the case of imprinted nanoparticles due to the existence of functional layer and pores within ion-imprinted film coated on electrode surface which leads to the specific binding sites for Pd(II). Besides being quite well selective to Pd(II) over interfering metal ions in buffer solution, the ratiometric fluorescence and electrochemical nanohybrid sensors were further tested for the determination of Pd(II) in spiked tap and artificial seawater samples. Acceptable outcomes were gained from the applications of the nanohybrid sensors for the correct detection of the Pd(II) amount loaded into the real water samples. The received results demonstrate that applying both ratiometric fluorescence and electrochemical methods could assure a reasonable recovery rate and standard deviation for routine analysis and detection of Pd(II) in real water samples.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Taner Arslan received his bachelor and master degree from Istanbul Technical University in 2014 and 2019, respectively. He is currently working as a R&D Manager at Ignis Nanotech, Inc. His research interests focus on imprinted polymers, quantum dots, wastewater treatment polymers, MOFs and macrocyclic molecules.

Sevgi Güney has been working in Department of Chemistry in Istanbul Technical University (ITU). She received her Ph.D. in analytical chemistry in 2010 from ITU. She has been currently working as an associated professor since 2016. Her research interests are development of electrochemical sensors for target metal ions or potentially bioactive molecules. She works on constructing of molecularly imprinted materials for electroanalysis.

Orhan Güney obtained his PhD degrees in Chemistry from Istanbul Technical University (ITU), Turkey in 1997. He worked on development of imprinted gels as a visiting scientist and postdoctoral fellow in Toyoichi Tanaka’s group in Physics in Massachusetts institute of Technology (MIT) and then, he returned to ITU where he is currently working as a professor since 2016. His research interests include the design and construction of ion-selective fluoroprobes, and development of imprinted polymers as chemical sensors.
